response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been thinking about this topic quite a bit lately, especially since I spend time on social media just a few times weekly. As someone who's not too young anymore, I remember when the internet was new and all the talk was about it being a great thing for connecting people. But now, seeing so much fake content—especially with my depression sometimes making me feel isolated—it can be overwhelming.

I think one of the best ways to deal with this is by staying informed but not taking everything at face value. I've learned through knitting and reading that it's important to question things, especially when they seem too good or too emotional to be true. Like when I see a news post about something big happening, I try to fact-check it right away using sites like BBC or Google News.

Another thing is to limit the time I spend online. Being unemployed and living in Bologna as an immigrant widens my world without children around me might help focus on real life more. My hobbies include cycling outside which feels much better for dealing with fake news than scrolling endlessly on social media.

I also find it helpful to engage less but be more mindful when I am online. That means not sharing everything too quickly because even though Christianity teaches us about discernment, some things aren't worth my time anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, consider the user's background: a middle-aged man with depression who has moderate openness and conscientiousness scores. Second, analyze his current situation in Bologna as an immigrant widower with hobbies like cycling to emphasize real-life engagement over digital one. Third, derive strategies from these traits: questioning information (knitting analogy) helps because he is open but not impulsive; limiting social media use aligns with moderate conscientiousness seeking structure.
